MLEM on MEG — Bonnaire2026 (surprisal × frequency, and feature contributions)

The story

Problem. MLEM = RSA with a learned metric: it fits weights over feature distances to predict neural-pattern distances (Spearman), then ranks features by permutation importance (PFI). It needs high-SNR neural representations.

Iterations (panel A). On single-trial MEG word responses MLEM is dead (fit ρ≈0.005 — the 306-d euclidean distance is all noise). Pseudo-trial averaging — bin words, average the MEG → high-SNR pseudo-items — is the fix: ρ climbs to ~0.18 (Route A), ~0.27 (Route A′, orthogonalised features), ~0.33 (8-feature prototypes).

Contrast verdict (panel B). Does distraction change the surprisal/frequency split? Supervised decoding answers yes — Task increases unique surprisal (⊥ frequency), r 0.095→0.109, paired p=0.006. MLEM cannot resolve it in any variant (PFI/W p=0.3–0.8): it gives one coarse, winner-take-most scalar per subject — underpowered for a small modulation.

Conclusion (panel C). Use decoding for the condition contrast; use MLEM for what it is built for — jointly ranking many features. There, the ~340 ms MEG word geometry is shaped mainly by syntactic constituent structure (n_closing, height, depth) and word frequency, not single-word surprisal.

Full maths, test history and roadmap: mlem/MLEM_REPORT.md.

Summary — iteration arc, contrast verdict, conclusion

Surprisal × frequency partition, Task vs NoTask (decoding + commonality + MLEM)

Route A — pseudo-trial MLEM (the SNR fix)

Route A′ — orthogonalised pseudo-trial MLEM (PFI + learned W)

Multi-feature contribution — PFI / W / simple-RSA / collinearity
